data.title: Enteq Shares Plunge on Cash Warning
data.description:
By Maitane Sardon

Shares in Enteq Technologies sank after the company said it may run out of cash earlier than expected amid mounting costs related to its Saber drilling technology.

Shares fell 61% to 1.20 pence in early trading, marking its lowest ever share price. Shares are currently down 89% over the past 12 months.

The specialist energy services company said a recent demonstration of its Saber tool at the Catoosa test facility in Oklahoma showed operational functionality, but identified several areas for improvement.

Enteq said it is working to begin active test drilling operations in April, but that the delay adds uncertainty to the revenue timeline as well as further costs.

"The timing of revenue previously expected to be earned is now more uncertain, and there will be costs associated with the engineering," the company said. "The current cash runway, based on current cash resources, is now expected to be shorter than previously outlined at the time of the 2024 fundraise."

Enteq continues to actively engage with potential customers, it said.

Write to Maitane Sardon at maitane.sardon@wsj.com

data.title: Upbeat Barclays names top picks for utilities sub-sectors in Europe
data.description:
** Barclays is positive on European utilities, with regulated utilities taking the top spot among sub-sectors ahead of vertically integrated names

** "We are in the foothills of a utility super-cycle," it says, expecting 2025 to represent an earnings trough ahead of future growth

** It sees possible upside surprises from balance sheet clarity, low European expectations and flexible generation, including introduction of capacity payments in Spain

** Its preferred names in regulated networks are National Grid , E.ON , Elia , Pennon and Redeia

** Among integrated utilities, its top picks are Enel and SSE

** Renewables is at the bottom of its sub-sector list, with RWE as the preferred play, with generators also among the less preferred, with Drax as top pick

** All the preferred stocks are rated "overweight"

** Barclays cuts EDP to "equal weight" from "overweight", saying the expected volatile performance of its renewables unit EDPR , also "equal weight", will be a key driver for the stock

data.title: Market Chatter: Hong Kong Court Orders Winding Up of China Evergrande Unit
data.description:
A court in Hong Kong ordered China Evergrande unit CEG Holdings BVI to wind up during a hearing on Friday, Bloomberg News reported the same day.

CEG Holdings BVI holds nearly half of Evergrande unit Evergrande Property Services , according to Bloomberg.

The liquidation petition, filed in September 2024, is part of a series of efforts by court-appointed liquidators to recover assets from Evergrande, the world's largest indebted developer, the report said. Evergrande's liquidation led to a downfall in China's property market.

Liquidators are also scrambling to recover $6 billion in dividends and cashback to seven persons including founder Hui Ka Yan, Bloomberg reported.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Rwanda Inflation Rate Hits Over 1-Year High
data.description:
Rwanda's annual inflation rate picked up to 6.4% in December 2024, the steepest since November 2023, accelerating from 3.4% in the prior month.

Food and non-alcoholic beverage prices rebounded, rising 5.7% compared to a 0.5% decline in November.

Additional upward pressure stemmed largely from prices of transportation (18.8% vs 16.7%) and communication (16.1%, the same rate as in November).On a monthly basis, consumer prices declined by 1.6% in December, after a 0.9% increase in the previous month.

data.title: Stocks wobble ahead of US jobs data; bonds in the spotlight
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. stock futures fall after market closure
  • Volatility ebbs ahead of critical U.S. payrolls data
  • Dollar towers near 2-yr peaks, up for 6th straight week

Global stocks were under pressure on Friday ahead of a U.S. jobs report later on that could exacerbate or ease the sell-off in the global bond market, while the pound headed for a fourth daily drop, after British debt yields soared to 16-year highs.

Volatility was more subdued in early European trading as traders stuck to their positions ahead of the upcoming employment data after this week's gyrations across markets.

European stocks got off to a limp start, with the STOXX 600 mildly in negative territory, as gains in telecoms and basic materials offset losses in more defensive sectors, such as utilities and consumer staples.

Nasdaq futures and S&P 500 futures were down 0.3% to 0.4%, indicating a softer start on Wall Street later, where markets closed overnight to mark the funeral of former U.S. President Jimmy Carter.

The closely watched U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 8:30 a.m. U.S. Eastern time (1330 GMT) is forecast to show a rise of 160,000 in jobs in December, while unemployment holds at 4.2%.

Anything stronger could see 10-year Treasury yields spike to 13-month peaks and lift the U.S. dollar in the process.

Analysts at ING believe a result below 150,000 new jobs would be needed to stop Treasury yields from rising further.

"Payrolls, as always, are a pivotal report. But we need to deviate materially from consensus to have an effect this time around," said Padhraic Garvey, regional head of research, Americas, at ING.

"Given the move already in Treasuries, there is some talk that Friday's numbers will need to be strong to continue this momentum, and in that sense there is some vulnerability for a lower yield reaction to a consensus outcome."

In Asia, Japan's Nikkei fell 0.9%, taking its weekly loss to 1.6%, while the MSCI index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) closed 1.2% lower on the week.

The VIX volatility index , a measure of investor nervousness, was flat on the day in European trading, having touched a three-week high earlier this week, when anxiety about the rise in global long-term bond yields peaked.

FED CAUTION

Fed officials Patrick Harker, the president of the Philadelphia Fed, and Kansas City President Jeff Schmid signaled they did not believe the central bank needed to cut rates imminently.

This had little bearing on market pricing, as traders have already only priced in around 43 basis points of U.S. rate cuts for 2025. Concerns about President-elect Donald Trump's potentially inflationary agenda have helped set these expectations and have been at the heart of this week's rise in long-term bond yields.

The benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose 2 basis points to 4.6998%, below Wednesday's eight-month peak of 4.73%. Traders are watching the 4.739% mark, as a break above here could trigger a rise to 5%, a level not seen since 2007.

This week's 9.6 basis point rise in Treasury yields has helped push the dollar to a sixth weekly rise. In sharp contrast, UK gilt yields (GB10YT=RR) have risen nearly a quarter of a percentage point to around 4.8%, their highest since 2008, which has weighed on the pound .

The pound fell for a fourth day on Friday, dropping 0.1% to $1.22915, having hit its lowest since November 2023 overnight, as concern has mounted over Britain's finances in light of the sharp increase in government borrowing costs, which outweighed the appeal of higher returns on UK assets.

A strong U.S. payrolls report could dent sterling further, according to XTB research director Kathleen Brooks.

"A strong payrolls report could add to the selling pressure on UK bonds and increase fears of a fiscal crisis in the UK. It could also weigh on the pound, which has been one of the weakest performers in the FX market since the start of this year," she said.

In commodities, oil prices rose on Friday, with Brent crude futures up 1.2% to $77.81 a barrel, while European natural gas prices (TRNLTTFMc1), fell 2.9%, set for a near-9% fall this week.

Gold prices headed for a 1.4% weekly rise, trading around $2,677, close to its highest since December.

data.title: Arise enters into a facilities agreement with DNB and announces early redemption of green bonds
data.description:
Arise AB

Arise AB (publ), ("Arise" or the "Company"), announces that the Company enters into a facilities agreement regarding a green term facility of approximately EUR 52 million and a green revolving facility of EUR 40 million with DNB and that the Company will redeem all outstanding bonds in the Company's EUR 50 million senior unsecured green bond 2022/2026 with ISIN SE0017487416 (the "Bond").

The Company has today, as borrower and security provider, entered into a facilities agreement regarding the refinancing of the Company’s green facility of approximately EUR 52 million and a new revolving green facility of EUR 40 million with DNB. In connection herewith, the Company has resolved to fully redeem the Bond in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Bond (the “Terms and Conditions”), which is financed with the Company’s existing liquid funds.

The new agreement entails a significant reduction of the Company’s financing costs as the Bond, with a cost of EURIBOR + 5.25%, is repurchased. The revolving facility runs with a margin against EURIBOR of 2.25%, and only on the utilized amount. The total annual financing costs are estimated to decrease by approximately EUR 2.5 million with an unused revolving facility. Additionally, the revolving facility increases the flexibility for the Company’s capital allocation compared to the Bond. A break-fee for the repurchase of the Bond and dissolution of previous arrangement fees will incur a one-time cost in the first quarter 2025 of approximately EUR 1.6 million.

The redemption of the bonds will take place on 31 January 2025 with a record date of 24 January 2025. The redemption amount corresponds to 102.625 per cent of the nominal amount per outstanding bond under the Bond along with accrued and unpaid interest in accordance with the Terms and Conditions. The redemption amount together with accrued and unpaid interest up to and including the redemption date will be distributed to each person who at the end of the business day on 24 January 2025 is registered as a bondholder in the debt register maintained by Euroclear Sweden.

In connection with the redemption, the Bond will be delisted from Nasdaq Stockholm Sustainable Bond List.

A notice of early redemption will be sent today to all bondholders according to the debt register maintained by Euroclear Sweden as of 3 January 2025.

“Securing a revolving facility of EUR 40 million while repurchasing the bonds is a testament to our strong financial position and track record. Flexible and cost-effective financing ensures that we are well-positioned to continue delivering profitable growth”, says Markus Larsson, CFO, Arise AB.

Halmstad 10 January 2025

ARISE AB (publ)                                                                                                

For further information, please contact:

Markus Larsson, CFO Arise AB, +46 735 321 776

This information is such information as Arise AB (publ) is obliged to make public pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation. The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the contact person set out above, at 10.30 CET on 10 January 2025.

About Arise

Arise is  a leading independent company that realises new green energy. The company develops, builds, sells and manages renewable electricity production. The company is listed on Nasdaq Stockholm.

Arise AB (publ), Linjegatan 7, SE-301 50 Halmstad, Sweden, telephone 46 (0)10 450 71 00, corporate id .no. 556274-6726. E-mail info@arise.se, www.arise.se

data.title: AEON Mall's Attributable Profit Drops Nearly 17% in Fiscal Q3
data.description:
AEON Mall's profit attributable to owners of the parent dropped 16.5% to 12.3 billion yen in the fiscal third quarter ended Nov. 30, 2024, from 14.7 billion yen a year ago.

The company's earnings per share (EPS) fell to 53.95 yen from 64.63 yen a year ago, according to a Thursday filing.

Revenue, however, gained 6% year on year to 332.7 billion yen from 313.9 billion yen.

It forecasted attributable profit to fall 19.1% to 16.5 billion yen or 72.50 yen per share and revenue to gain 7.2% to 453.5 billion yen for the fiscal year ending Feb. 28, 2025.

The company expects to pay a year-end dividend of 25 yen per share for the current fiscal year.

AEON Mall's shares shed nearly 5% at market close.

data.title: Market Chatter: Update: Saudi Arabia to Increase Crude Prices for Asia In February
data.description:
(Updates to add Saudi Aramco's statement)

Saudi Arabia will reportedly increase the price of crude oil for the Asian market in February, Reuters reported Monday, citing a pricing document from Saudi Arabian Oil (SASE:2222), d/b/a Saudi Aramco.

The state-owned oil and gas giant will increase the flagship Arab Light crude's official selling per-barrel price by $0.60 month over month to $1.50 in February.

Additionally, the company hiked its crude prices in Northwest Europe and the Mediterranean by $1.30 per barrel, while it slashed its US prices by $0.30 to $0.40.

Saudi Aramco declined to comment when reached by MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: India's EPACK Durable falls after large deals at discount
data.description:
** Shares of air conditioner manufacturer EPACK Durable down the exchange-allowed maximum of 5% to 568.45 rupees, set to fall for a third straight session

** Overall, 673,821 shares traded in 5 block deals on NSE at a discount ranging between 4.7% to 5% - LSEG data

** Trading vols at 1.6 mln shares, more than 2x the 30-day average

** EPAC set to snap 6-week gaining streak, down ~9% during the week

** Stock more than doubled since its trading debut in January last year

data.title: Thermo Fisher Scientific Says Olink Platform Chosen by UK Proteomics Project
data.description:
Thermo Fisher Scientific said late Thursday the UK Biobank Pharma Proteomics Project has chosen its Olink Explore Platform to support a human proteomics study.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

The project will analyze more than 5,400 proteins from 600,000 samples to help find new biomarkers that could potentially identify, diagnose and treat diseases, the company said.

The project is set to begin in Q1.

data.title: Court Lifts Seizure on Hubei Geoway Investment Subsidiary's Properties
data.description:
Hubei Geoway Investment said the court has lifted the seizure of properties owned by subsidiary Bozhou Zongxiang.

The property consisted of 179 units in a commercial building at 200 Shuanghe Road, Fuyang City in China's Anhui Province, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Shares of the internet-of-things equipment maker closed 1% lower Friday.

data.title: Insurers and luxury
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 down 0.2%
  • Luxury leads, utilities lags
  • Wall St futures lower

INSURERS AND LUXURY

It's been a pretty quiet first hour of trading with the STOXX 600 and most national benchmarks trading pretty near flat.

But there are a couple of sector stories in the mix that are enough to keep things busy until U.S. payrolls later.

Insurers, particularly those with large exposure to the wildfires menacing Los Angeles, are down with Beazley, Lancashire and Hiscox all down around 3%, the three biggest fallers across UK-listed large and midcaps. (.FTLC)

Then a report from an Italian financial daily that Italy's Prada is looking at Versace, could be helping luxury names in case it sparks a new wave of deal making. The European luxury index is up 0.6% the biggest sectoral gainer today.

Eyes are also on Ubisoft - nothing new there - down 7% at the bottom of the STOXX, aafter the video game maker postponed "Assassin's Creed Shadows" premiere again and cut its Q3 and FY net bookings outlook.

(Alun John)

*****

data.title: Sterling Likely to Recover Later in Year on BOE Caution — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling could remain under pressure in the near-term on concerns about higher U.K. borrowing costs but is likely to recover later in the year, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. "A likely more cautious rate-cutting stance from the Bank of England versus its peers--as well as an eventual softening of the dollar's overvaluation--still supports a sterling revival to $1.34 by year-end," they say. With volatility and a weaker sterling set to persist in the near-term, investors could find value in structured strategies that generate income or gradually accumulate sterling until fiscal policy uncertainty eases and investor confidence rebounds. Sterling falls 0.1% to $1.2291. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Catcher Technology Buys Back Shares Worth NT$156 Million
data.description:
Catcher Technology repurchased 791,000 shares of the company worth NT$155.9 million, according to a Taiwan Exchange filing on Thursday.

The shares were bought at an average price of NT$197.09, and account for 2.57% of the company's total issued shares.

data.title: DHL Unit Buys Inmar Intelligence's Reverse Logistics Business in US
data.description:
Deutsche Post unit DHL Supply Chain said Friday purchased Inmar Supply Chain Solutions, a division of Inmar Intelligence specializing in returns for the online retail industry.

The acquisition will expand the logistics giant unit's footprint in North America with the addition of 14 return centers and over 800 associates, and enhance its return capabilities through the inclusion of product remarketing, recall management and supply chain performance analytics. It will also contribute towards DHL's 2050 decarbonization goals.

Inmar Intelligence's pharmaceutical reverse distribution business was not part of the acquisition.

data.title: Atalaya Mining Finalizes Re-Domiciliation to Spain; Name Changed to Atalaya Mining Copper
data.description:
Atalaya Mining completed its re-domiciliation to Spain from Cyprus, according to a Friday release.

The copper producer will now trade under its new name, Atalaya Mining Copper.

data.title: Cellnex Hires JPMorgan to Explore Sale of Its Stake in Swiss Subsidiary, Expansion Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• Cellnex hired U.S. bank JPMorgan as an advisor to explore the potential sale of its 72% stake in its Swiss subsidiary, Spanish newspaper Expansion reports, citing unnamed sources.
  • The Spanish telecommunications-infrastructure company could raise more than 1.1 billion euros ($1.13 billion)in proceeds from the sale, Expansion says.
  • Cellnex declined to comment, the report says.

Full story: https://tinyurl.com/58uuce7j

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0532 GMT - Tencent's overall business fundamentals and growth outlook remain intact despite recently being added to a Pentagon list with alleged Chinese military ties, OCBC analysts write in a note. However, the news may indicate rising geopolitical tensions and could be a share-price overhang until further clarity on the development, they say. Tencent's key businesses are value-added services and online advertising, which remain positive over the long term. The recent share-price selloff may be an overreaction and provides for a long-term accumulation opportunity, they say. OCBC maintains a buy rating on the stock at fair value of HK$560.00. Shares are last at HK$373.00. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0419 GMT - Xiaomi's core businesses likely remained resilient in 4Q of 2024, OCBC analysts write in a note. The company was a "star performer" last year as share prices more than doubled on year, they say. The strong share performance was driven by solid growth in its core businesses on resilient margins and stronger-than-expected EV business margins, they say. Xiaomi's smartphone segment gross profit margin may bottom out in 4Q with potential improvement ahead given higher average selling price and lower component cost, they add. OCBC maintains its buy rating for the stock with a fair value at HK$40.50. Shares last at HK$33.60.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0319 GMT - Thailand's telecom sector is likely to face some headwinds in 2025-2026, CGS International's Weerapat Wonk-Urai says in a research report as the brokerage downgrades the sector to neutral from overweight. Headwinds include slower organic growth and potentially higher regulatory risk owing to upcoming spectrum auction, the analyst says. Also, the sector's valuation of 3.4X estimated 2025 price-to-book-value ratio, which is two standard deviations above its six-year historical average, looks expensive to CGS. The brokerage maintains hold ratings and target prices of THB287.00 and THB12.70 for Advanced Info Service and True Corp., respectively. Advanced Info Service shares are 0.4% higher at THB277.00; True Corp. shares are 0.9% lower at THB10.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0120 GMT - The U.S.'s planned restrictions on artificial intelligence chip exports are expected to have limited direct impact on Malaysian technology companies, given their minimal role in the global AI supply chain, RHB IB analyst Lee Meng Horng says in a note. While indirect effects are harder to gauge, the local tech sector's exposure remains low, he reckons. Lee remains optimistic about a stronger 2025, driven by a sector recovery supported by robust broad-based demand and replacement cycle. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the Malaysian tech sector and pegs Malaysian Pacific Industries, CTOS Digital, Coraza Integrated Technology and Datasonic Group as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1723 GMT - The U.K.'s Competition watchdog underestimates the havoc that could be wreaked by letting browsers that rival Safari operate on iPhones and iPads without relying on Apple's WebKit engine, Apple says in response to the regulator's investigation into mobile browsers and cloud gaming. "The importance of mandating a high base level of stability, security, and privacy should not be understated," the iPhone maker says. A panel of experts told the CMA in November it should consider investigating Apple and Google's business practices under the U.K.'s new digital competition rules. Alphabet's Google also says in response that the watchdog's findings don't account for evidence that shows there is strong browser competition on Android devices. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1420 GMT - The food-delivery sector's road to recovery won't be linear, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. Significant progress has been made in the industry, with companies proving they can be free cash generative, they say. However, the sector still faces challenges. German online delivery company Delivery Hero's switch to an employment model in Spain is expected to hit adjusted Ebitda by around 100 million euros in 2025. Meanwhile, Deliveroo and Just Eat reported 3Q order growth around 1%-2% points below consensus in the core U.K. and German markets. "The past few months have felt somewhat of a false dawn for a rejuvenation of sentiment in food delivery," the U.S. bank says. Deliveroo shares are up 0.9%. Delivery Hero and Just Eat shares are down 1.4% and 2%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1301 GMT - Weak sentiment and negative news flow around NAND memory chips will likely put pressure on VAT's medium-term growth outlook, Jefferies analyst Olivia Honychurch says in a research note as it downgrades the Swiss valve maker to hold from buy. A DRAM memory chip slowdown in 2026 will add to VAT's woes, as DRAM is the company's second most important revenue driver via its three largest customers --Lam Research, Tokyo Electron and Applied Materials--. This should consequently limit VAT's share-price upside this year, Jefferies says. The investment bank expects VAT to cut its full-year guidance in March. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

1147 GMT - ASM International should book a steady stream of orders from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. over the next four to five quarters, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. Order from TSMC, the world's largest contract chip maker, should help the Dutch supplier of semiconductor tools meet or beat order expectations, they say. Intel, Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix and Micron Technology are also expected to place more orders, the analysts add. They anticipate ASM International will report 800 million euros in orders for the fourth quarter. ASM International shares trade 0.6% lower at 590.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1126 GMT - ASML Holding is expected to report higher orders starting in the first quarter compared to recent months, thanks to large clients like Samsung Electronics and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Jefferies analysts write in a research note. The analysts expect the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker to post orders of 3.6 billion euros for the fourth quarter of 2024. However, they say these should climb to a range of 5 billion euros to 6 billion euros from the first quarter of 2025. The analysts cited an increase in orders from TSMC as well as the prospect that Samsung might soon be able to supply its high bandwidth memory chips to Nvidia as potential boosts for ASML. ASML shares trade 0.5% lower at 720.80 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1108 GMT - European semiconductor share prices this year will mainly depend on investors' confidence in 2026 revenue and earnings growth prospects, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. They note that demand in major end-markets like PCs, smartphones, general servers, consumer electronics and networking remains weak. "We believe the weaker than previously expected outlook for 2025 is largely priced in for most European semiconductor stocks," the analysts say, pointing to 2026 as a more important year for the sector. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1053 GMT - Talabat Holding's business is still growing, with many growth opportunities looking ahead, Barclays analysts write in a note. Delivery Hero's Middle East subsidiary Talabat has strong leadership positions across several markets, supported by a well-executed strategy, the analyst say. "Beyond restaurants, it has grocery at 24% gross merchandise value and broader retail at 1% GMV driving incremental users and frequency," they add. Talabat's business is very profitable, with further margin expansion potential, they say. The U.K. bank initiate its stock recommendation at overweight and a target price at 2.0 UAE dirham. Shares are down 0.6% at 1.63 dirham. ( najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1016 GMT - Telecommunication companies are well-placed for another positive year in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. The sector showed its strength last year, outperforming the broader market, the analysts say. "Over the previous 10 years, the sector was a consistent underperformer, with only three years of outperformance," they add. However, resilient pricing, declining capital expenses and the return of M&A activity will likely support momentum, the U.S. bank says. Shares in Deutsche Telekom, Swisscom, and BT are expected to perform well in 2025, though French telcos such as Orange and Bouygues look less attractive, they add. BT and Deutsche Telekom shares are up 0.8% at 144.90 pence and 0.5% at 29.24 euros, respectively. Swisscom shares are flat at 508 Swiss francs. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0545 GMT - Zydus Lifesciences' earnings are poised to rise on higher sales contribution from Sitagliptin 505b(2) drug, Nomura analysts say in a research report. Assuming Sitagliptin market at $300-$500 million at a discounted price, the opportunity size for Zydus Lifesciences could be as big as $100-$150 million annualized, the analysts say. The brokerage lifts its earnings estimates for the Indian pharmaceutical company by 1% for FY 2025, 6% for FY 2026, and 6% for FY 2027. It raises the stock's rating to buy from neutral and the target price to INR1,140.00 from INR1,030.00. Shares are 0.4% higher at INR1,013.10. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1821 GMT - Biotech's year ahead looks uncertain, but it could still be a safer option in a market downturn, Baird analysts say. The unknowns of a Trump administration, including possible tariffs and regulatory changes, make it hard to predict industry growth, though analysts are cautiously optimistic. If inflation and volatility worsen, analysts believe it could revamp investor confidence in large-cap biotech companies as a safe place to keep their money. If tariffs and inflation are better than expected, small and mid-sized companies could benefit from a lower cost of capital. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1122 GMT - The U.S. Food and Drug Administration's request for a label warning about Guillain-Barre syndrome risk on GSK's respiratory syncytial virus shot Arexvy could negatively impact vaccine sales, Bryan Garnier Research analysts say in a note. While a causal link between vaccination and GBS-- a rapid-onset muscle weakness caused by the immune system-- hasn't been proven, seven GBS cases per million Arexvy doses were observed, according to the FDA. "We believe the observed GBS incidence very close to natural GBS incidence in the non-vaccinated population," the analysts say. Arexvy sales already declined double-digit in 2H of 2024, following the narrowed recommendation to a smaller age range and more at-risk patients, the analysts say. Shares fall 0.04% at 1359.50 pence.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0721 GMT - BBVA's reduction of the acceptance level of its $12 billion-plus hostile bid to take over Banco de Sabadell is mostly a technical adjustment at the request of Spanish regulators, RBC Capital Markets' Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say in a research note. BBVA said its offer requires more than half of the effective voting rights of Sabadell, excluding treasury shares. Regulators saw a need to clarify what BBVA's acceptance threshold was and the Spanish bank's move effectively means the minimum acceptance is reduced by 0.7%, according to RBC. "We continue to be of the view that this deal gets done, probably by [the first half], and probably with some relatively minor remedies and a 10% increase in the offer price," the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0238 GMT - Current market expectations and trading multiples have set a very high bar for Australia's banks in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. "While multiples have remained elevated for many months, we think a de-rating is likely in the year ahead," they say. As share prices stand, meaningful upgrades to consensus EPS and dividend forecasts appear to already be priced in. "There is very little margin for error," say the analysts. They prefer nonbank financial stocks in Australia this year. Among major Australian banks, MS's order of preference is overweight-rated NAB, followed by equal-weight-rated ANZ, and then underweight-rated Westpac and CBA. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0120 GMT - The U.S.'s planned restrictions on artificial intelligence chip exports are expected to have limited direct impact on Malaysian technology companies, given their minimal role in the global AI supply chain, RHB IB analyst Lee Meng Horng says in a note. While indirect effects are harder to gauge, the local tech sector's exposure remains low, he reckons. Lee remains optimistic about a stronger 2025, driven by a sector recovery supported by robust broad-based demand and replacement cycle. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the Malaysian tech sector and pegs Malaysian Pacific Industries, CTOS Digital, Coraza Integrated Technology and Datasonic Group as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1719 GMT - The increase in U.K. bond yields to levels not seen since 1998 will boost U.K. pension providers' solvency by increasing the discount applied to personal liabilities, says Mark Capleton, an analyst at BofA Securities. "This should be generally speaking quite favorable for pension schemes," Capleton says. The yield on the 30-year bond or gilt, as they are called in the U.K., hit 5.455% earlier Thursday, while the yield on the 10-year gilt rose to 4.921%, before stabilizing in afternoon trading. The selloff has been more gradual than in October 2022, when the long-term bond yields rose on the back of the U.K. mini-budget under the leadership of former U.K. Prime Minister Liz Truss, Capleton says. Market liquidity is much more normal nowadays and the daily moves are more modest, he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1512 GMT - The Mid-Atlantic and Florida offer the best opportunities for first-time homebuyers, a Realtor.com survey says. Harrisburg, Penn., tops the list, followed by Rochester, N.Y., and several Florida cities including Villas, Lauderdale Lakes, and Altamonte Springs. These markets were selected for affordable listings, robust local economies, family-friendly environments, and investment potential. With national asking rents declining for 16 months, buying has become more appealing, especially where housing costs 30% or less of income. In these top markets, housing expenses range from 15.2% of prospective homebuyers' income in Rochester to 24.8% in Wilmington, with median listing prices below the national median of $416,880. The most expensive market is Villas, Fla., which has a median listing price of $236,950. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1422 GMT - KPMG's outlook for M&A activity into 2025 is improving among consumer and retail companies. Inflation is moderating and higher wages support muted consumer spending growth, while a more accommodative financial environment should allow for further interest rate cuts, KPMG says. "As a number of uncertainties in the market have been mitigated in the past several months, it seems that M&A waiting game is over," according to KPMG. That said, KPMG believes transactors need to remain patient and disciplined in approaching the market. Particular attention should be focused on deal activity aimed at business objectives, long-term thinking and developing a detailed plan to realize deal value.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1322 GMT - Merger and acquisition activity during 3Q reflected a decline in volume, but a lift in value, KPMG partner Frank Petraglia says in KPMG's latest report on M&A in the consumer and retail industry. The total number of deals declined 13% on a sequential basis to 505, while the value of the deals increased dramatically by 402% to $59.4 billion, driven by Mars' purchase of Kellanova for $35.9 billion. "KPMG is encouraged by the third-quarter deal activity as consumer and retail firms engage in transactions that strategically align with profitable growth," Petraglia adds. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1143 GMT - Canada's housing market saw a mixed finish to 2024, with the strong fall market rally losing a lot of steam as resale transactions fell markedly in Calgary and Toronto between November and December. Royal Bank of Canada's Robert Hogue notes the drop in Toronto rolled back all advances made since September, though the soft patch wasn't seen across the country and a recovery is still on track in British Columbia's Vancouver and Fraser Valley and transactions remain near all-time highs in Edmonton, Alberta, despite edging lower in December. The economist believe further interest rate cuts by the Bank of Canada will stimulate homebuyer demand in 2025, which will keep the rally going in most markets even if strained affordability and federal cuts to immigration restrain the pace. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1212 GMT - Barclays believes that Nordic bank share prices could be driven by capital return announcements, 2025 guidance and net interest income in upcoming fourth quarter reports. It thinks net interest income consensus expectations are too high for Swedbank, SEB and Danske Bank, but believes consensus net interest income and EPS expectations for 2025-2026 are too low for DNB Bank. "We believe DNB is best placed into fourth quarter 2024 numbers relative to Swedbank and SEB." Danske Bank may drop its full-year 2025 net interest income guidance, Handelsbanken consensus dividend estimates are too low but fourth quarter 2024 consensus net interest income is too high, and Nordea consensus fourth quarter net interest income and dividends are too high. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1141 GMT - Deutsche Bank is expected to launch a share buyback of 1 billion euros at its fourth-quarter results later this month, Barclays's Flora Bocahut and Krishnendra Dubey say in a research note. A buyback could be an important milestone for the German bank, which hasn't repurchased stock since it completed a 675 million-euro program in July, the analysts says. Deutsche Bank is likely to report continuing revenue momentum for the fourth quarter, especially in investment banking, according to Barclays. After negative surprises in each of the second and third quarters, provisions will return to more normal levels, the analysts say. Shares rise 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1102 GMT - CVC Capital Partners has limited room to surprise this year after a strong share performance since its initial public offering in April, Barclays's Michael Sanderson and Krystal Han say in a research note. The Amsterdam-listed buyout group is set to continue raising investors' funds, but it will take time before that translates into revenue generation, the analysts say. Moreover, consensus expectations for 2025 already point to a sharp step up in CVC's carried interest and performance fees, the analysts add. Barclays lowers its recommendation on the stock to equalweight from overweight and increases its target price to 22 euros from 21.80 euros. Shares fall 0.2% to 21.32 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1019 GMT - Partners Group Holding looks set to benefit from its exposure to the U.S., where opportunities in both investment exits and fundraising are expected to arise this year, Barclays's Michael Sanderson and Krystal Han say in a research note. "We see Partners Group as the best positioned in our private capital coverage to benefit from likely 2025 conditions and we upgrade our rating to [overweight]" from equalweight, the analysts say. Shares in the Swiss private-equity group could rise as higher transactional activity provides evidence of an improvement in conditions, the analysts add. Barclays also lifts its target price on Partners Group to 1,470 Swiss francs from 1,120 francs. Shares rise 1.8% to 1,321 francs. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0218 GMT - The progress of Dialog Group's Baram Junior Cluster field development is progressing positively and could strengthen the company's domestic upstream portfolio, RHB IB analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The project recently received a final investment decision, with an estimated capital expenditure of US$235 million. Following the approval, Dialog is set to begin a two-year development phase, Lim notes. The project is targeting first gas production by end-2026, and could contribute approximately MYR40 million annually to Dialog's earnings, assuming a project internal rate of return of 12%, he adds. RHB raises Dialog's target price to MYR3.11 from MYR3.09, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.5% lower at MYR1.83. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2054 GMT - An artificial intelligence-fueled boom of data-center construction is lifting demand for electricity and creating opportunities for developers of tools that can help make power systems more resilient to weather-related disruptions, says Jay Koh, a managing director at Lightsmith Group in New York. The private-equity firm last year invested in artificial-intelligence startup AiDash, whose software helps utilities keep trees away from power lines to reduce wildfire risks. "As you see increasing volatility due to extreme weather events, whether it's fire risk or storm risk or flood risk or heat waves, we think that there's going to be enormous amounts of demand for resilience of the energy system," Koh says. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

2148 GMT - Australian stocks are expected to open higher, with ASX futures up 0.3% before the bell. Australia's benchmark index lacks typical cues from Wall Street, where U.S. stock markets were closed for the day to mark former President Jimmy Carter's memorial. The S&P/ASX 200 edged 0.2% lower Thursday, snapping the five-day winning streak it recorded to start the new year. Firmer commodity prices may be a tailwind for some mining and energy stocks, with iron ore, gold, copper and crude oil all gaining. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2001 GMT - Oil futures settle higher amid signs of strong demand, including seven straight weeks of U.S. inventory draws and prospects of higher heating fuel use. Although last week's 1 million barrel crude storage decline reported by the EIA was smaller than the API's 4 million barrel estimate, "it still was a decline," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "The negatives that remain are builds in storage for gasoline and diesel inventories along with a very strong U.S. dollar index," he says. February WTI remains technically bullish above key moving averages, with resistance around $75.29-$76.41, Kissler adds. WTI settles up 0.8% at $73.92 a barrel, and Brent gains 1% to $76.92 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1315 GMT - Oil futures rise after retreating yesterday from 3-month highs in a selloff driven by large builds in U.S. product inventories. "Some wind has come out of crude's sails over the last couple of sessions," Pepperstone's Michael Brown says in a note. "The near-term outlook hinges largely on the same factors that drove the market last year, chiefly the demand outlook, and whether the Chinese economy is at long last able to embark on a stable and durable economic recovery." Geopolitical risks are likely to limit downside for now, he adds. WTI and Brent are up 0.6% at $73.73 and $76.58 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1406 GMT - Shell's weak fourth-quarter trading update broke its streak of strong earnings but its underlying performance and cash generation remain solid, analysts at HSBC write. The U.K.-based energy giant said Wednesday that integrated gas trading is expected to be "significantly lower" in the fourth quarter due to expiring hedging contracts. The one consolation for investors is that the losses will only impact earnings and not cashflow, the analysts write. There is little read-across from Wednesday's trading update to Shell's peers given most of the negative elements were company specific, they write. However, the sector's earnings momentum could remain negative over the near term, they add. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1337 GMT - Shell will be able to maintain its share buyback rate and continue delivering returns to shareholders even if the price of oil drops significantly, UBS analysts write. The London-based energy giant has the safest buyback record in the sector given its low dividend break-even point of $36 a barrel, the analysts write. If oil prices were to significantly deteriorate, which the analysts don't expect, then Shell would be the last in the sector to cut buybacks, they write. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1314 GMT - Shell is undervalued by investors given it has the best buyback record in the sector and a strong balance sheet, UBS analysts write. Shares in the London-based energy giant are down 10% since July despite its restructuring and retreat from green-energy investments, the analysts write. Shell can surpass its $2-3 billion savings target by the end of the 2025 and has the opportunity to make an additional $6 billion in cost savings, UBS says. Shell's valuation gap with its U.S. peers has narrowed to around 50%, and it could fall further as its renewed focus on oil and gas assets delivers results, they write. UBS upgrades the stock rating to buy from neutral and increases its target price to 30 pounds. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1044 GMT - Repsol shares look set to rise as refining margins rebound, UBS analysts write. The Spanish energy major's stock has fallen 12% in the past six months and investors fail to account for higher gas prices and Repsol's earnings momentum, the analysts write. The analysts increase their earnings per share forecasts by 17%, putting them 8% ahead of consensus expectations. Repsol shares could be boosted by buybacks worth around 800 million euros in 2025, a UBS forecast that is nearly 150 million euros ahead of consensus expectations. A strong balance sheet and asset sales across should also boost shares, they write. UBS upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, and increases its target price to 13 euros from 11.5 euros. Shares trade up 2.1% at 12.04 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1003 GMT - Eni's share price has been boosted by asset sales and buybacks but this means there is now limited room for positive surprises, analysts at UBS write. Since mid-June, Eni shares have outperformed peers by 8% but it will be increasingly hard for the oil major to beat market expectation. This could cap its share-price growth, the analyst write. Investors are unwilling to place high-value on green-energy investments and the absence of future oil price rises means Eni's earnings per share momentum might be stalling. UBS downgrades the stock's rating to neutral from buy. Shares trade down 0.1% to 13.66 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0218 GMT - The progress of Dialog Group's Baram Junior Cluster field development is progressing positively and could strengthen the company's domestic upstream portfolio, RHB IB analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The project recently received a final investment decision, with an estimated capital expenditure of US$235 million. Following the approval, Dialog is set to begin a two-year development phase, Lim notes. The project is targeting first gas production by end-2026, and could contribute approximately MYR40 million annually to Dialog's earnings, assuming a project internal rate of return of 12%, he adds. RHB raises Dialog's target price to MYR3.11 from MYR3.09, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.5% lower at MYR1.83. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0057 GMT - Posco Holdings could post below-consensus 4Q earnings on narrower profit margins, Daishin Securities analyst Lee Tae-hwan writes in a note. The South Korean steelmaker may have sequentially recovered in sales, which likely topped 8.4 million metric tons of steel in volume for the October-December period. However, its profit margins may have weakened due to lower steel-product prices, Lee says. He estimates Posco's 4Q operating profit at KRW474 billion, well below the market consensus forecast of KRW698 billion. Daishin cuts its target price for the stock by 13% to KRW410,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 1.0% lower at KRW258,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2148 GMT - Australian stocks are expected to open higher, with ASX futures up 0.3% before the bell. Australia's benchmark index lacks typical cues from Wall Street, where U.S. stock markets were closed for the day to mark former President Jimmy Carter's memorial. The S&P/ASX 200 edged 0.2% lower Thursday, snapping the five-day winning streak it recorded to start the new year. Firmer commodity prices may be a tailwind for some mining and energy stocks, with iron ore, gold, copper and crude oil all gaining. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1838 GMT - Front-month gold futures close up 0.7% to $2,683.80 an ounce. It's the third day in a row that gold moved higher, this despite indications from the Federal Reserve that only one rate cut may come in 2025, which the market takes as a negative for assets like gold. "Fed minutes on Wednesday said that 'almost all participants judged that upside risks to the inflation outlook had increased,'" says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. Gold managed to shake this off, as did most commodities with the exception of some agricultural and soft commodities. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1148 GMT - Ivanhoe managed a strong finish to 2024, though the Canadian miner's production guidance for the new year was lighter than expected, says Raymond James' Farooq Hamed. The analyst says 4Q production at Ivanhoe's Kamoa-Kakula operation in Congo was better than anticipated, driven by higher throughput with the phase 3 mill development. Still, he adds 2025 copper guidance included a wide range, with the midpoint lower than expected. Hamed suggests the conservative range reflects the potential for power availability issues to impact operations. Raymond James retains an outperform call on the shares, but trims its target by C$1 to C$24. Ivanhoe was last at C$16.92, up 27% over the last year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

data.title: UK's Serco falls on Jefferies downgrade
data.description:
** Shares of British outsourcer Serco fall as much as 2.8% to 147.2p, stock among top pct. losers on FTSE 250 (.FTMC) index

** Jefferies analysts downgrade rating on SRP to "hold" from "buy"; cut target price to 175p from 225p

** Jefferies says near-term earnings and free cash-flow momentum looks more muted for Serco, given challenges in its largest contracts

** "Immigration contracts and UK national insurance have been a headwind to Serco shares" - Jefferies

** Brokerage says solid underlying end-market fundamentals, particularly in U.S. defence, will not be enough to offset announced or expected headwinds in key contracts and UK National Insurance

** Eight of 12 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 3 "hold" and 1 "sell"; median PT 210.42p – LSEG data

** Stock fell ~7% in 2024
